tagged_story_for_tts:
Invisible waves. Voices without wires.

The word radio comes from radius, Latin for "spray" or "beam"—fitting, since radio sends signals spraying outward like ripples in a pond. But unlike water, these waves are silent, invisible, and move at light’s speed: 299,792 kilometers per second.

Radio starts with electricity vibrating inside a transmitter, pushing electrons up and down an antenna. This motion sprays electromagnetic waves into the air, just as a pebble tossed into water creates spreading rings. A receiver’s antenna catches these waves, reversing the process: vibrations become sound. No cables. No delay.

Early radios used spark gaps—two metal rods with a tiny air gap. When high-voltage current jumped the gap, it created bursts of static-laden waves, like a Morse code drumbeat. By 1906, Reginald Fessenden transmitted the first voice and music (a violin solo) on Christmas Eve, proving radio could carry more than dots and dashes. Ships at sea heard it through crude headphones, their operators stunned by a disembodied orchestra.

Today, radio tunes cars to stations, guides planes via radar (which bounces radio waves off objects), and lets astronauts phone Earth from 400 kilometers up. Even Wi-Fi and Bluetooth are radio’s descendants—just tighter, shorter waves.

Three related subjects are sound as energy, how antennas shape signals, and light as a wave.
